subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clearly stated, narrowly focused research question referenced throughout the text
Logical sectional alignment that communicates the topic with adequate clarity
Relevant definitions of key constructs (social media platforms, adolescent age range, disorder types)
weaknesses: Methodology description is brief with limited detail on source selection or reliability
Repetitive phrasing and unfinished headings (âIntroducciâ, âRelaciâ) distract from coherence
Overall tone remains largely descriptive rather than critically investigative
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction and question formulation demonstrate clarity and focus, placing this work in the mid-band (3â4). However, methodology lacks detail about databases, selection criteria and source reliability, and the presence of truncated headings and a descriptive tone prevent a higher mark. A score of 3 reflects solid performance that nonetheless falls short of fully effective informed selection and seamless communication.

strengths: Demonstrates sound factual knowledge of adolescence, social-media algorithms and eating disorders
Accurate use of key terms (e.g., anorexia nerviosa, algoritmo, thinspo)
Integration of empirical data (e.g., Internet quality agency statistics) adds weight to the argument
weaknesses: Reliance on popular-media or NGO sites limits academic rigour
Frequent paraphrasing rather than synthesis reduces depth of understanding
Occasional misused terms (e.g., âmalformaciÃ³nâ) and spelling/heading errors detract from precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and understanding are generally clear and supported by relevant sources, placing this work firmly in the 3â4 band. The student uses subject-specific terminology appropriately and displays coherent understanding, but over-reliance on less scholarly sources and lapses in precision prevent a top-band mark. A score of 3 accurately reflects good but not consistently excellent performance.

strengths: Logical connections made between algorithmic exposure, body-image ideals and psychopathology
Wide range of references (â60) largely pertinent to the research question
Conclusion acknowledges limitations and revisits the hypothesis
weaknesses: Analysis remains descriptive in places with limited methodological critique
Conclusions rely on cited figures without deeper interrogation of data validity
Evaluation of evidence and alternative explanations is only partially developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay progresses beyond mere description, offering clear analysis and a reasoned argument within the 4â6 band of Critical Thinking. Research is mostly relevant and the discussion shows some critical evaluation, but a lack of deeper methodological critique and a more robust evaluation of sources keeps it at the lower half of the band. A score of 4 reflects these strengths and limitations.

strengths: Follows a recognisable Extended Essay structure with title page, table of contents and references
Consistent referencing style and clear separation of âReferenciasâ section
Pages are numbered and word count/title details are provided
weaknesses: Multiple truncated headings (âIntroducciâ, âRelaciâ, âConclusiâ) interrupt flow
Inconsistent page numbering in the contents table
Typographical artifacts in headings and split research question formatting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall organisation meets core conventions (3â4 band), but numerous lapses in heading completeness, page numbering and minor typographical errors reduce the polish. A score of 2 reflects adequate presentation that does not consistently achieve all formatting and structural conventions.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological and clinical terms (anorexia, bulimia, algoritmo, alfabetizaciÃ³n mediÃ¡tica, corteza prefrontal, thinspo) are defined and used largely correctly. Occasional spelling problems and unfinished headings detract from precision, but conceptual use remains mostly accurate and appropriate. This performance sits comfortably within the 3-4 band.

Word count, title and session details are provided; referencing style is mostly consistent; pages appear numbered; headings are clearly formatted. Minor typographical issues and occasional unfinished accents reduce polish but layout still meets expected EE standards, fitting the 3-4 band at a score of 3.

The student demonstrates solid factual knowledge of adolescence, social-media algorithms and three specific eating disorders, supported by numerous citations. However, many citations are popular-media or NGO sites, and source material is frequently paraphrased rather than synthesised. The understanding is clear but not consistently critical or original, fitting the 3-4 band at a score of 3.

The essay relies on secondary sources gathered from academic journals, government sites and several journalistic webpages. The student briefly explains that information was "buscada, recopilada, interpretada, analizada y sintetizada," but offers little detail about databases, selection criteria or source reliability. The range is adequate but not especially broad or critically chosen. This reflects a mostly complete but not fully robust methodologyâappropriate for the mid-3 band within 3-4.

The introduction states a clear hypothesis, defines key constructs (social-media platforms, adolescent age range, eating-disorder types) and situates the issue in a contemporary context. Section headings and subsequent content stay reasonably aligned with that stated focus. Minor weaknesses include repetitive phrasing, some unfinished headings ("Introducci", "Relaci"), and a largely descriptive tone, but overall the topic and purpose are communicated with adequate clarity. This aligns best with the 3-4 band.

Roughly 60 references are listed, many directly connected to social media and eating-disorder prevalence. While academic depth varies, the majority are pertinent to the question, and statistics are generally linked back to adolescent females. Some sources of dubious scholarly weight lower academic rigour, keeping the strand in the mid-range of the 4-6 band.

The research question â "Â¿En quÃ© medida las redes sociales de TikTok e Instagram afectan a la creaciÃ³n de los trastornos de la conducta alimentaria en mujeres adolescentes de entre 12 y 15 aÃ±os?" â is explicitly stated, narrowly targets two platforms and a specific demographic, and is referenced throughout the body and conclusion. Although the analysis sometimes drifts into general discussion of eating disorders, the linkage to the stated question remains visible, placing the work at the lower end of the 5-6 band.

The essay follows a recognisable EE structure with title page, table of contents, sequential sections, conclusion and references. Page-number errors in the contents table and some truncated headings show lapses, yet overall organisation meets core conventions. This earns a solid 3 in the 3-4 band.

A logical argument is presented, culminating in a conclusion that revisits the hypothesis and acknowledges some study limitations (secondary data, cross-cultural generalisation). Evaluation of evidence credibility and alternative explanations is brief, leaving the discussion only partially critical. This positions the work toward the lower end of the 4-6 band.

The essay moves beyond description by connecting algorithmic exposure, body-image ideals and psychopathology; however, causal links are asserted more than critically examined, and data are seldom statistically interrogated. Conclusions typically rest on cited figures without deeper methodological critique. This demonstrates clear but limited analysis, meriting 4 within the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the studyâs focus on social media and eating disorders, guiding reader expectation.
The âIntroducciâ heading is truncated; complete it as âIntroducciÃ³nâ to meet academic presentation standards.
The research question is split over two lines and contains an OCR artifact (âa\tilde{n}osâ); ensure full continuous formatting with correct special characters for clarity.
The word count is explicitly provided and complies with IB formatting requirements.
The table of contents lists âConclusiÃ³nâ and âReferenciasâ as page 1, causing inconsistency; correct page numbering to reflect actual document structure.
Consider numbering the âTabla de contenidosâ header consistently with IB conventions to improve structural clarity.
The description of physical changes during adolescence is detailed and well-supported by credible sources.
The âRedes socialesâ section effectively transitions the focus toward digital platforms and sets up subsequent analysis.
Consider expanding the bulimia section with statistics on prevalence among 12â15-year-olds to strengthen relevance.
The âRelaciâ heading is incomplete; correct to âRelaciÃ³n entre redes sociales y trastornos de la conducta alimentariaâ to clearly signpost the analysis section.
The prevalence statistic is presented descriptively; critique the study methodology or consider discussing confidence intervals to deepen analysis.
The methodology description is brief and broad; include specific details on databases searched, selection criteria, and source reliability to strengthen methodological rigor.
The DSM-5 is cited as published by the OMS; can the student clarify the correct publisher (American Psychiatric Association) to ensure accuracy?
The text on interpersonal relationships is informative but lacks connection to the research question; explicitly link how identity crises on social media platforms influence disordered eating.
Heading hierarchy is inconsistent (h4 for section 3); use consistent heading levels to avoid confusion and improve navigation.
The definition of anorexia nerviosa is precise and supported by a recent clinical source.
The bulimia description ends mid-sentence due to truncation; ensure full paragraphs are presented to maintain argument flow.
The discussion of algorithmic personalization is descriptive; explicitly link how these mechanisms exacerbate risk factors for eating disorders to strengthen argument.
The term âmalformaciÃ³nâ seems misused in the context of misinformation; replace with âmala informaciÃ³nâ or âdesinformaciÃ³nâ for precision.
The âConclusiâ heading is truncated; revise to the full word âConclusiÃ³nâ for consistency.
References show inconsistent formatting (e.g., missing italics for journal names); apply a consistent citation style throughout.
Integration of data from Internet quality agency adds current, empirical weight to the argument about harmful content growth.
The paragraph highlights misinformation risk but could be improved by referencing actual examples of harmful TikTok posts to illustrate points.
Discussion of hashtags like #Ana and #Mia shows a practical understanding of how social media culture influences TCA trends.
Evaluate the reliability of the âAgencia de Calidad de Internetâ data by checking original methodology details to substantiate claims.
The conclusion summarizes points but lacks critical evaluation of limitations; include a brief discussion on potential biases or unexplored variables.
The âReferenciasâ section is clearly titled and separated from main text, adhering to academic conventions.
Several URLs break awkwardly across lines; reformat references so each entry appears on a single line to improve readability.
The Luiza Drubscky reference includes a URL with encoded characters; simplify or hyperlink correctly according to the chosen style.
The DOI is embedded within a math tag; ensure DOIs are formatted as plain text or hyperlinks to adhere to IB guidelines.
